title: Benchmark-Conditional Robustness Audits for Schema and Entity Matching
description: This project proposes a benchmark-evaluation methodology for perturbation robustness in data integration. On one schema-matching benchmark and one entity-matching benchmark, it defines conservative benchmark-specific admissibility rules, audits both accepted and rejected perturbations, and tests whether robustness conclusions materially change relative to naive perturbation protocols.
motivation: Prior work already shows that entity-matching systems can be brittle under label-preserving perturbations, so the main open problem is not discovering more perturbations. The real gap is methodological: robustness studies in data integration often lack explicit benchmark-relative criteria for which perturbations still preserve the released label, especially once latent benchmark-specific evidence and ambiguity structure are considered.
proposed_approach: Instantiate an Audited Benchmark-Conditional Admissibility protocol on T2D-SM-WH and one fixed WDC Products pairwise slice. For each benchmark, define protected evidence, benchmark-specific normalizers, competitor-aware admissibility checks, conservative perturbation families, and reason-coded accept/reject decisions; then audit 120 accepted and 120 rejected perturbations, including a non-author annotator on a stratified subset, and compare robustness conclusions against format-validity-only and unrestricted perturbation protocols under a CPU-feasible search budget.

related_work.title: Probing the Robustness of Pre-trained Language Models for Entity Matching
related_work.how_it_differs: This is the closest prior work and already studies label-preserving perturbations for entity matching. The proposed paper does not claim novelty over perturbation-based EM robustness testing; it contributes benchmark-conditional admissibility, deterministic checker logic tied to released benchmark evidence, and a dual-sided audit over both accepted and rejected perturbations.

related_work.title: Bridging the Gap between Reality and Ideality of Entity Matching: A Revisiting and Benchmark Re-Construction
related_work.how_it_differs: That work reconstructs entity-matching benchmarks to better reflect reality. This proposal keeps the released benchmarks fixed and asks how perturbation robustness should be evaluated responsibly on top of those fixed labels.

related_work.title: Valentine: Evaluating Matching Techniques for Dataset Discovery
related_work.how_it_differs: Valentine is an evaluation framework for dataset matching techniques and is important methodological grounding for benchmark-aware comparison. The proposed paper extends that evaluation mindset to robustness auditing under benchmark-specific admissibility constraints.

related_work.title: The Knowledge Graph Track at OAEI -- Gold Standards, Baselines, and the Golden Hammer Bias
related_work.how_it_differs: This paper is about ontology and knowledge-graph matching evaluation, not data-integration perturbations, but it directly motivates the proposal's emphasis on gold-standard construction, hidden benchmark bias, and conservative evaluation protocols.

related_work.title: A Survey of Approaches to Automatic Schema Matching
related_work.how_it_differs: This survey is foundational schema-matching literature and explains why matching decisions depend on multiple fragile signals. The proposed paper uses that insight to argue for benchmark-specific protected evidence rather than benchmark-agnostic perturbation rules.

related_work.title: Generic Schema Matching with Cupid
related_work.how_it_differs: Cupid is a classic hybrid matcher relying on names, types, and structure. It supports the proposal's argument that schema-matching labels can change under subtle evidence shifts, which is why admissibility must explicitly preserve protected evidence and nearby competitor structure.

related_work.title: Stress-Testing ML Pipelines with Adversarial Data Corruption
related_work.how_it_differs: That paper studies targeted corruption search for general ML pipelines. This proposal uses only a small CPU-feasible search and argues that data-integration robustness requires a benchmark-preservation layer before such search is interpretable.

related_work.title: WDC Products: A Multi-Dimensional Entity Matching Benchmark
related_work.how_it_differs: This is the entity-matching benchmark used by the proposal. It provides the benchmark design and official slices, but not a benchmark-conditional admissibility or audit protocol.

hypothesis: If perturbation robustness is evaluated only on benchmark-specific perturbations that preserve released benchmark evidence, avoid creating new benchmark-relevant ambiguity, and pass a dual-sided audit, then robustness conclusions will either change materially relative to naive perturbation protocols or reveal that the defensible perturbation space is much narrower than standard robustness studies assume.

confirm: Accepted perturbations achieve at least 0.95 audit precision.
Rejected perturbations have at least a 0.70 violation rate, showing that the checker blocks many genuinely label-threatening cases.
Two-author inter-annotator agreement reaches at least Cohen's kappa 0.70.
A non-author annotator agrees with adjudicated labels at least 0.75 on the stratified external-validation subset.
At least one benchmark shows either a method-ranking reversal or an absolute worst-case F1-drop difference of at least 8 points between the audited protocol and a naive protocol.
Targeted search inside the admissible space is stronger than matched random admissible sampling under the same CPU budget.
All experiments finish within the 2-core, CPU-only, roughly 8-hour budget.
refute: Accepted perturbations frequently fail to preserve the released benchmark label.
Rejected perturbations are often still label-preserving, implying the admissibility rules are badly targeted or overly conservative.
External validation materially disagrees with the adjudicated audit labels.
The checker requires repeated benchmark-specific exceptions to stay viable.
informative_negative_result: If accepted precision is high and rejected violation rate is moderate or high, but ABCA and naive perturbation protocols differ by less than 3 absolute F1 points in worst-case drop and produce no ranking reversals on either benchmark, the paper will be framed as a cautionary empirical study showing that conservative admissibility does not materially change conclusions on these benchmarks.

If the overall acceptance rate falls below 15% while audit precision remains high, the paper will be framed as evidence that some released benchmarks support only a very narrow defensible robustness test space.
